sentences.text: When I came to Bucharest, at that time, you were not authorized to-- to live in Bucharest without a special authorization, which naturally I didn't have.
So I had to sneak in illegally into Bucharest.
Once you were there, you were there.
But you couldn't go in there where Romanian Army around the highways, and they were asking everybody for such a permit.
The youth movement-- the Zionist youth movement at that time was full of initiative and very badly organized.

But we were a bunch of youngsters, idealistic people, and we tried to organize in all the places and cities of Romania groups of youths In some places, we organized [NON-ENGLISH], we called them, or preparatory branches.
And they-- there, we prepared for agricultural life in Israel.
We organized regional meetings.
We organized summer camps.
We didn't organize any winter camps.
We didn't have this idea at that time.

And there was such a fraternity, first of all, within the youth movement, and despite competition between the youth movements, because we had all one goal-- somehow to leave this cursed land of Europe and go to Israel.
Naturally, at that time-- whoever knows history-- the British had the White Book.
And according to the White Book, they didn't authorize people to come to Israel.
So the [INAUDIBLE], the Haganah started to struggle [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] against this White Book and to organize task force of Jews into Palestine.
By the way, it wasn't only the Haganah.
There was also the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the right-wing organization, which had even started earlier to organize such little ship.
But on a big scale, there was mainly the Haganah.
At the beginning, they were sent to a place, Atlit.
They had a camp in Israel next to Haifa-- to Atlit.
But then they decided that they would send all these who came to Israel without certificates, as they call that-- they would be deported to camps in Cyprus.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I'll come to that.
And they ended up in these ships.
And either they went to Atlit or, later on, to Cyprus.
And there was the famous event or the famous [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] episode of which was a ship going from France somewhere, and has been sent back.
That was worse than to be deported to-- to Cyprus, because on Cyprus, at least a number of Jews were, every month-- they were left-- left to-- they were authorized to go to Palestine.
I think it was 1,500 Jews a month.
And from Europe, it was just to go back to those places where everybody wanted so badly to leave.
Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then two big ships were organized by the Haganah in Romania.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was supposed not to go.
But my father didn't want to go alone.
He was on the lists.
He was called to be on the list.
He was on the list to go.
And he said, we made-- I made sure with my son.
All these troubles in-- in Transnistria and coming back-- and so all-- I do not go without him.
And he went to the authorities of the Haganah in Bucharest and fought out that I go also with.
So my father was at that time about 50 years old.
And my mother, my three children-- their-- their three daughters and their son, they went on illegal-- illegal immigration.
And we went Motza'ei Shabbat from Bucharest by train to a place called [INAUDIBLE], which is all the Donau, on the Danube, which holds a very wide river.
And in the night, we were shipped over to the Bulgarian coast in a place called Rusciciuk, R-U-S-C-I-C-I-- Rus-- --C-I-U-K.
And I even remember the song that I sang at that passage on the Donau, which is talking about a little ship floating on the-- on the waters.
And then we came at this-- at the end.
And by the way, on my shoulder was this little Sefer Torah that I carried with me.
I carried it back to my city, to my little town.
I took it when I went to Israel.
I took it with me.
And this was my [INAUDIBLE], they said.
And there, we had to walk a long tunnel-- I remember that we were exhausted-- until we finally saw the wagons of the Bulgarian railways.
In the middle each wagon, there was bread, and water, and wonderful grapes.
And I immediately made the comparison between the same wagons many years ago, when we went and we were deported.
And these trains brought us to a place called Burgars.
It's a port.
B-U-R-G-A-R-S.
Excuse me.
And there, at the end-- at the eve of Yom Kippur, 1947-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we arrived there day by train around 5 o'clock in the morning.
And during two or three hours, 2,400 people were squeezed in in a little boat like sardines.
They had-- everybody had to lie down until everybody's on ship.
And immediately after this has been done, the ship started to-- to-- to take off.
And I'll never forget these two-- the majority of these people standing on the deck.
There wasn't where to sit or whatever.
And on the shore, the members of the Haganah that organized that, with the Bulgarian officers and non-Jewish officer, singing together the 'Hatikvah.' That is a picture I'll never forget.
But we didn't go too far.
We just went a little bit in the Black Sea outside of the territorial waters of the Bulgaria, because the Bulgarians didn't want-- they had-- they were afraid that the British are going to intervene.
So we wanted to be outside of their territories.
But we had to wait a second ship that had to be loaded.
And they didn't manage to reach before Yom Kippur.
So they had to stop in the field somewhere over Yom Kippur.
And only Motza'ei Yom Kippur they came in.
And they packed up this other little ship.
It was about 1,600 Jews.
And so we were two ships on the Black Sea, sailing for-- for Eretz Israel, and 4,000 people in them.
If you would have seen the ships-- it was the 'In-tri-pid,' or something like this.
There was a kind of an-- one was an icebreaker, but OK.
And we started to sail together until-- by the way, next day, we all took all of our documents that we had.
We tore them up.
We threw it into the Black Sea.
And we are now new people, newborn people, all of us-- free people going to Eretz Israel.
And until we came to Turkey, we had to go through the Bosphorus, and then to-- through the Marmara Sea, and we came to the Dardanelles.
And as we crossed the Dardanelles into the Mediterranean, the-- the Aegean Sea to the Mediterranean, there was already a British destroyer waiting for us.
British hospitality is renowned, so they sent a destroyer to greet us.
And we sailed, and the destroyer became a-- our company.
And then there were two destroyers.
And then there were more destroyers.
We ended up with eight destroyers and two little ships.
So we had a [INAUDIBLE] of our own--
--in that transport.
And so we reached the-- the-- we got near to Eretz Israel.
And we were about 40 miles from Israel, still not in the Israeli-- the-- the British-- at that time, it was British Mandate-- territory-- territorial waters.
Two destroyers attacked us.
They just closed our way.
And oh, they didn't know one thing-- that our ship was a icebreaker.
So he, the-- the-- the captain of the ship went into one of the destroyers to make a huge hole.
They to-- to-- to tow away this destroyer.
But they immediately started to throw [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tear bombs, tear gas-- bombs with tear gas.
And naturally we all had tears in our eyes and there's burns everywhere.
And the only thing that we did, we could do, was we all sang the 'Hatikvah'-- once, twice, three times, four times, for endless.
And suddenly, there was dark, because the Haganah people, they-- they destroyed the engine, everything.
There was no electricity, no ventilation, no nothing.
But we continued to inhale this tear gas and to sing the 'Hatikvah.' And naturally, they came on the boat.
And they towed us away with another destroyer into Haifa.
And it was again-- I don't know how it came-- there was a [NON-ENGLISH]-- same day as we were deported from home to Transnistria, the same day we were deported from Haifa to Cyprus.
The evening of Hoshana Rabbah.
'Barb-led?'
Barbed wires.
And so they brought us to Famagusta, which is a port in Cyprus.
We landed Hoshana Rabbah in the morning, in Famagusta.
They brought us by British cars to a new established camp in the-- in our honor called Camp number 70 which was all tents.
And everybody somehow found the tent for the family.
The ground was sandy, so the tents didn't keep well.
And God gave us-- in-- on the first night or the second night-- I don't remember-- a wonderful wind and rain.
And all the camps start to-- to vanish.
And we could watch the sky.
We woke up, and watching the sky.
And we couldn't find which was our tent and what was happening to us.
And that was Yontif.
[INAUDIBLE] was Yontif, and you couldn't go [INAUDIBLE] the tent.
But that was-- we went over that also.
And we started to organize our life in this camp.
We started to organize a synagogue.
And the Sefer Torah became again a center of Jewish life in Cyprus.
My father, who was a very clever man, a real clever man-- intelligent person and erudite in many ways-- he became their judge of the court of the camp.
The court-- the camp had a-- a court, and he became judge.
And he also performed all kinds of things concerning the [? natural ?] life, lectures-- all kind of cultural life in that camp.
But as to myself, because I was a youth leader, there was a special camp called [INAUDIBLE], the-- the Village of Youths.
So the-- the leading of the-- the leader leading the direction of these camps, which was a Jewish direction, they asked the permission to transport me from one to another camp.
So there came a British car and brought me over to Camp 64.
And there, I acted a number of months.
And then in 19-- in Camp 68, another youth village has opened, a religious youth village.
And I became the director of that youth village at that time.
The few short episodes of that time, one which is humoristic, the seven-- the-- the Camp 70 was all Romanians.
And the stereotype of Romanians are that they are thieves.
So they Haganah struggled to open up over the wires bridges so that we can go from one camp to the other.
When they finally succeeded, they announced on the radio, on the loudspeaker, hooray, hooray, the-- the gate is open-- close the doors.
[LAUGHTER] Then there, I experienced the vote in the United Nations.
It was a night-- an unforgettable night.
All people of the camps went out with covers of pots, with all kinds which makes noise.
And we started to walk around the fence, around the barbed wires, and yelling, Tommy, go home!
There was the British soldiers who were guarding us.
Tommy, go home!
We have a country!
And all kinds of things.
They immediately called up the tank-- the tanks of the British army.
And they surrounded-- they thought they were going to make a rebellion, something like this.
They surrounded all the camps.
But that was a night we were walking in pajamas and in clothes that was nightgowns--
--everywhere you could see.
Nobody stayed home.
And it was really some experience.
There, I learned a lot, because there was a seminar for instructors.

And we learned a lot of Bible, of Jewish history, all kind of things, in the most informal way by very great, erudite-- one of them was a-- a-- a man by the name of Yakov Katz, professor-- he became Professor Yakov Katz and was director of the Hebrew University.
And we used to eat some-- what food we had there, we ate together in the same dining hall.
And then in 1948--
--Golda Meir came to Cyprus.
And the Jewish Agency, who was the leading organization of the Jewish people at that time, they got a special authorization from the British to take out 10,000 children from the camps.
If I may be wrong with the number, if it's 10,000 or 8,000-- thousands of children.
And she came to us to convince us that we should leave, let the Haganah determine a given number of people to go with that transport because they were interested in having soldier in Israel.
Her speech was unforgettable.
It was in Yiddish.
She was then carried like the pope.
And we loved her.
I-- since then, I loved her.
And these transports started to organize.
So in-- in order to-- to earn a place that I could take as a [HEBREW], as a counselor-- I was young-looking-- younger than today at that time.
So they shaved me, and they shaved my legs and everything, and they gave me some short pants.
And I became this youngster of [INAUDIBLE] that I told you before.
And I went as a child to-- to Palestine.
I arrived-- How old were you supposed to be?
I arrived-- I arrived the 31st of March or April 1, 1948-- about six, seven weeks, because before, the state of Israel has been established.
I arrived to Tel Aviv, which was already under Jewish administration.
The port of Tel Aviv couldn't even take in such a big ship.
It didn't-- a big ship that we were transported.
And so we were shipped by little boats from the ship to the coast.
And there, I changed my name back to Yaacov Rand, but I didn't take-- I wasn't-- I didn't take care to change my age.
And then I started to have problems, because I was already at 20 years old, and not 17 years old.
So I had to go to the military court and to declare my real age.
And here, I made a mistake.
And I put in 26 of November, 26, where in reality, I was born November 1.
I knew that much, much later.
Immediately after arriving to Israel, I arrived with a group of youngsters, of children.
We went to Ra'anana, and from there, to Karkur in order to organize all these children within the educational framework of youth aliyah.
And immediately, I enrolled into the army.
I had to enroll as a volunteer because at that time, I was still 17 years old.
So in order to volunteer into the Israeli army.
I served in the army since May 1948 until February 1951, and at that time, as a lieutenant.
And later on, as a reserve officer, I finished 30 years later my army service as a captain.
I was an infantry soldier-- at least, a regular soldier.
I was a deputy commander of a platoon-- platoon.
I was in communication.
I became-- I was trained in communication-- electronic communication, Morse communication.
I knew to-- to play with-- with this keys.
And then I was into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] armors?
How do you call it?
Tanks.
Armors.
I don't know these expansions in English.
And then I was in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] information, intelligence, and ended up ultimately-- I was also in artillery.
And then I ended up as anti-air artillery.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Took some time to get there.
Now, naturally, when I left the army.
Meanwhile, my wife came over from Romania.
We weren't married, but-- but-- I am sorry.
My-- my former bride came over from Romania.
She's my former bride, isn't she?
And I started to work.
But somehow, I said to myself, time is coming to study.
So how can you study when you work and you want to marry?
It doesn't go together.
So I started to study [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by correspondence.
So I went to the Ministry of Education in order to be matriculated as a student.
So they asked me, how about public school?
Honestly, I said, I never went to public school.
They said, you don't have any documents?
I said, I couldn't have a document.
I never went to.
They said, in that-- in that case, you have to first give the exams of the eighth grade.
So I had to give the exams of the eighth grade.
And then I had to give the-- the-- there are two parts of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] high school.
And I gave all these exams.
And I finished-- I got high school certification after 13 months.
So I do not understand why people have to study 12 years all these matters.
OK.
So I started.
And then I didn't study anything until I went to Paris as a delegate of Bnei Akiva for three years.
And in Paris, I got the lust to study.
And I came back.
I went to the Hebrew University.
And then I was 34 years old.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've worked in many places.
First of all, I worked in the intelligence, in Israeli intelligence, in counter-espionage.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, after three or three and a half years, I left and I became an educator in a village for mentally impaired and mentally disturbed children.
There I worked another three or four years.
And there I got my educational [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] taste.
And since then, since 1954, I'm in education until nowadays.
It makes already about 42 years.
After working there, I was sent as a delegate of the Jewish Agency to Paris to lead and to organize the movement of Bnei Akiva.
And I worked there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] close to three years.
There's one little thing which I feel it's-- gives me a lot of satisfaction.
Years later, one of the guys that I worked with them in Paris [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made an integration to his new house in the Old City of Jerusalem.
And he found, by moving from one place to the other-- he found the regulations of the first [INAUDIBLE] that I organized for aliyah signed by 20 members.
Out of these 20, 18 are living in Israel.
And that's, I think, something which is rare.
They are living and they're happy-- their families and their grandchildren, everything.
So that's a little satisfaction of this time.
And in 1960, I came back to Israel and started to work as a teacher in the agricultural school of Mikveh Israel.
